passage:
Text 1
Language teachers often debate how much time students should spend on explicit grammar instruction versus free conversation practice. Many traditional educators believe that learning grammar rules first gives students a solid foundation before they begin speaking. They argue that students who lack this foundation make errors that become hard to correct later.

Text 2
A study at a community college compared two groups of adult learners studying Spanish. The first group spent 60% of class time on grammar exercises and 40% on conversation. The second group reversed this ratio, spending 60% on conversation and 40% on grammar. After one semester, the conversation-heavy group scored 22% higher on speaking assessments, while both groups performed equally well on written grammar tests.
stem: The study in Text 2 most directly addresses the debate in Text 1 by showing that

choices.label: A
choices.text: more conversation practice improved speaking without hurting grammar scores.

choices.label: B
choices.text: grammar instruction is unnecessary for adult language learners.

choices.label: C
choices.text: adult learners need more grammar practice than younger students.

choices.label: D
choices.text: speaking assessments are more important than written grammar tests.

explanation: Text 1 presents the debate between grammar-first instruction and conversation practice, with traditional educators arguing for grammar first as a foundation. Text 2 directly addresses this by showing that the conversation-heavy group (60% conversation, 40% grammar) scored 22% higher on speaking assessments while still performing equally well on grammar tests. This means increasing conversation time improved speaking without sacrificing grammar knowledge — directly challenging the grammar-first argument in Text 1. Choice A accurately captures this synthesis.

distractorRationale.A: Correct. The study shows the conversation-heavy group improved on speaking (22% higher scores) while matching the grammar-heavy group on written grammar tests, undermining the claim that grammar instruction must come first.
distractorRationale.B: Text 2 never suggests grammar instruction is unnecessary — both groups still received grammar instruction, just in different proportions. The study shows balance matters, not that grammar should be eliminated.
distractorRationale.C: Text 2 only compares adult learners to each other; it makes no comparison between adults and younger students. This claim goes beyond what the study can support.
distractorRationale.D: Neither text makes a claim about the relative importance of different test types. Text 2 reports results from both types of assessments without ranking one above the other.
